Distress Tolerance Handout 6   (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 4)
TIP Skills: Changing Your Body Chemistry
To reduce extreme emotion mind fast.
Remember these as TIP skills:
T
Tip the Temperature of your face with cold water*
(to calm down fast)
âąâą Holding your breath, put your face in a bowl of cold water,
or hold a cold pack (or zip-lock bag of cold water) on your eyes and cheeks.
âąâą Hold for 30 seconds. Keep water above 50Â°F.

Intense exercise*
(to calm down your body when it is revved up by emotion)
âąâą Engage in intense exercise, if only for a short while.
âąâą Expend your bodyâs stored up physical energy by running, walking fast, jumping,
playing basketball, lifting weights, etc.

Paced breathing
(pace your breathing by slowing it down)
âąâą Breathe deeply into your belly.
âąâą Slow your pace of inhaling and exhaling way down (on average, five to six breaths
per minute).
âąâą Breathe out more slowly than you breathe in (for example, 5 seconds in and 7
seconds out).
Paired muscle relaxation
(to calm down by pairing muscle relaxation with breathing out)
âąâą While breathing into your belly deeply tense your body muscles (not so much as
to cause a cramp).
âąâą Notice the tension in your body.
âąâą While breathing out, say the word âRelaxâ in your mind.
âąâą Let go of the tension.
âąâą Notice the difference in your body.
*Caution: Very cold water decreases your heart rate rapidly. Intense exercise will increase heart rate. Consult your health care provider before
using these skills if you have a heart or medical condition, a lowered base heart rate due to medications, take a beta-blocker, are allergic to
cold, or have an eating disorder.

Paced breathing
(pace your breathing by slowing it down)
âąâą Breathe deeply into your belly.
âąâą Slow your pace of inhaling and exhaling way down (on average, five to six breaths
per minute).
âąâą Breathe out more slowly than you breathe in (for example, 5 seconds in and 7
seconds out).
Paired muscle relaxation
(to calm down by pairing muscle relaxation with breathing out)
âąâą While breathing into your belly deeply tense your body muscles (not so much as
to cause a cramp).
âąâą Notice the tension in your body.
âąâą While breathing out, say the word âRelaxâ in your mind.
âąâą Let go of the tension.
âąâą Notice the difference in your body.
*Caution: Very cold water decreases your heart rate rapidly. Intense exercise will increase heart rate. Consult your health care provider before

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: TIP Skills: Changing Your Body Chemistry
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Distress tolerance means getting through the wave without adding harm. Use gentle, medically safer versions first.
tiny_steps: Choose one body-safe skill for 30 seconds.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Replace intense exercise with hand squeeze, paced exhale, safe fidget, weighted comfort if tolerated, or visual grounding.
Avoid cold shock/breath-holding/intense exercise unless already clinician-approved and safe that day.
Keep oxygen, rescue meds, allergy plan, mobility aids, and exit routes accessible.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use crisis skills as punishment, sensory flooding, or exposure without consent.
